preamble: The shift from the voiced velar plosive [ɡ] to the voiceless uvular plosive [q] involves both retraction (back of tongue raising further back) and devoicing. It is a classic pathway of emphasis shift, particularly in Semitic languages where uvulars are emphatic correlates of their velar counterparts.

examples.note: The uvular [q] is the emphatic counterpart of [k]; however, in some modern dialects, /ɡ/ (as in loanwords) may be assimilated to [q] in emphatic contexts.

sources: Holes, C. (2004). Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties.
Watson, J. C. (2002). The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic.
